## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; BBAAX exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
  - Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
  - Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; BBAAX exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
  - Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
  - Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage
75	
76	The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.
77	
78	Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.
79	
80	**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.
81	
82	Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.
83	
84	Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.
85	
86	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
87	
88	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
89	
90	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
91	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
92	
93	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
94	
95	Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.
96	
97	**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.
98	
99	## Suite-level fail-fast
100	
101	Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.
102	
103	## No-teardown decision
104	
105	The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:
106	
107	- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
108	- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
109	- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
110	- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.
111	
112	Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).
113	
114	## Operator scope
115	
116	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.
117	
118	## Coupling to ₣A_
119	
120	Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.
121	
122	## Locked design decisions
123	
124	- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
125	- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
126	- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.
127	
128	## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh
129	
130	Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.
131	
132	**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.
133	
134	**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):
135	
136	- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
137	- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
138	- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
139	- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.
140	
141	Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.
142	
143	Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.
144	
145	## References
146	
147	- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
148	- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
149	- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
150	- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
151	- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)
152	
153	# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAX
154	
155	## Character
156	
157	First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; BBAAX exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
158	
159	**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
160	
161	Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
162	
163	## Cost
164	
165	~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
166	
167	## Scope
168	
169	- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
170	- Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
171	- Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
172	- Triage routing per surprise:
173	  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
174	  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
175	- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
176	
177	## Constraints
178	
179	- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
180	- **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
181	- Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
182	- Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
183	- Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
184	
185	## Cross-pace dependencies
186	
187	- gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
188	
189	## Out of scope
190	
191	- Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
192	- /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
193	- Multi-day re-run scenarios
194	- Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired
195

## Failure mode contract

## Release-branch execution contract

Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.

**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.

**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. **Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.

Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.

## Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

## Gauntlet test suite — design

`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.

2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)

Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.

The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.

Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.

Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.

## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing

Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:

- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.

**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.

Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.

**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.

## Suite-level fail-fast

Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.

## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions

- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.

## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh

Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.

Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.

Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.

## References

- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAX

## Character

First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; BBAAX exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.

**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.

Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

## Scope

- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
- Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
- Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
- Triage routing per surprise:
  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions

## Constraints

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
- **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
- Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
- Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
- Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue

## Cross-pace dependencies

- gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)

## Out of scope

- Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
- /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
- Multi-day re-run scenarios
- Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

lastPrompt: mount BB

The entry contract (clean tree, marshal-zero HEAD signature, blank RBRR, no RBRAs, no hallmarks, no depot-scoped vessel fields) is enforced by the test itself. Suite is the **gauntlet** (TestSuite-shaped composition, not a custom orchestrator) walking enrollment-validation → pristine-lifecycle → canonical-establish → canonical-onboarding-sequence → regimes → four-mode → per-crucible fixtures. Dual purpose: release qualification + handbook-regression coverage. Locked: depot-identity-collapse, cult-verb naming, build/run split.

**Name assessment:** "rbw-tP-first-run" fits the intent-only docket — keep.

- `rbw-tP` and let it run; capture every surprise verbatim
- Triage at each surprise: tiny inline (fix+re-run) vs larger (slate follow-up, surface to you)

**Execution strategy:** Sequential opus — high cost per attempt (~1 hour + 2 GCP projects), each surprise needs judgment routing (inline vs. slate), and any retry mandates full marshal-zero reset (no patch-and-continue per RELEASE.md failure-mode contract).

Ready to proceed? Worth confirming: (1) Payor OAuth is currently healthy, (2) we're on a release branch (current is `main` per gitStatus — this needs handling before marshal-zero per the Release-branch execution contract).

content.content:
RELEASE.md
# Release Procedure

The release qualification ceremony for the project maintainer — five operator steps, roughly one hour wall-clock, with cloud cost on the order of two GCP projects per run.
The ceremony exists to catch silent first-build assumptions that the routine `tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh` and `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` tiers tolerate by design.

[Payor](README.md#Payor) OAuth is the only prerequisite credential.
All other credentials — [Governor](README.md#Governor), [Director](README.md#Director), and [Retriever](README.md#Retriever) service-account [RBRA](README.md#RBRA) files — are minted by the qualification itself, not restored from backup.
The entire credential chain must be reproducible from a clean local state.

**Failure mode contract.** Mid-qualification failure means start over from step 2, not patch-and-continue.
Patching forward is exactly the bug class this ceremony exists to catch — accumulated state hides first-build assumptions.

## 1. Confirm Payor health

- [Refreshes](README.md#Refresh) the [Payor](README.md#Payor) OAuth token so subsequent [Payor](README.md#Payor)-authority operations do not fail mid-qualification on token expiry
- Use `tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh` instead if [Payor](README.md#Payor) credentials have never been [Installed](README.md#Install) on this workstation
- Success: the command exits clean
- Failure: re-[Establish](README.md#Establish) the [Manor](README.md#Manor) before proceeding — the ceremony cannot run without a healthy [Payor](README.md#Payor)

```
tt/rbw-gPR.PayorRefresh.sh
```

## 2. Marshal zero, then commit

The marshal-zero operation returns local state to a blank template:

- Blanks [Repo Regime](README.md#RBRR) fields ([Depot](README.md#Depot) identity, project ID, prefixes)
- Deletes all [RBRA](README.md#RBRA) credential files ([Governor](README.md#Governor), [Director](README.md#Director), [Retriever](README.md#Retriever))
- Blanks [Hallmark](README.md#Hallmark) pins in each [Nameplate](README.md#Nameplate) regime file ([RBRN](README.md#RBRN))
- Blanks depot-scoped fields in each [Vessel](README.md#Vessel) regime file ([RBRV](README.md#RBRV))

Then commit the result with the marshal-zero signature in the commit message — step 3 detects that signature on `HEAD` and refuses to run otherwise.

- Success: working tree clean and `HEAD` carries the signature
- Failure: review the marshal output for files the operation would not zero, resolve, and retry

```
tt/rbw-MZ.MarshalZeroes.sh
```

## 3. Run the pristine qualification

The release gate.

**Entry contract** — `rbw-tP` refuses to run unless ALL of the following hold:

- marshal-zero signature on `HEAD`
- working tree clean
- [RBRA](README.md#RBRA) files absent
- [Repo Regime](README.md#RBRR) fields blank
- [Hallmark](README.md#Hallmark) pins blank in every [Nameplate](README.md#Nameplate) regime ([RBRN](README.md#RBRN))
- depot-scoped fields blank in every [Vessel](README.md#Vessel) regime file ([RBRV](README.md#RBRV))

The contract is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline — that property is what lets this tier catch silent-first-build assumptions by construction.

**Cost** — ~1 hour wall-clock plus two GCP projects per run:

- one throwaway [Depot](README.md#Depot) created and torn down by the `pristine-lifecycle` fixture's `depot-lifecycle` case
- one canonical [Depot](README.md#Depot) [Levied](README.md#Levy) by the canonical-infrastructure setup phase
- GCP project deletion is asynchronous (30-day soft delete) — each run leaves pending-delete projects in the project list; this is accepted cost

**Sequence**:

- `pristine-lifecycle` fixture — marshal-zero gate plus idempotent throwaway lifecycles for [Depot](README.md#Depot), [Governor](README.md#Governor), [Retriever](README.md#Retriever), and [Director](README.md#Director)
- canonical infrastructure setup — [Mantle](README.md#Mantle) the [Governor](README.md#Governor) + deploy [RBRA](README.md#RBRA), [Charter](README.md#Charter) the [Retriever](README.md#Retriever) + deploy [RBRA](README.md#RBRA), [Knight](README.md#Knight) the [Director](README.md#Director) + deploy [RBRA](README.md#RBRA), [Levy](README.md#Levy) the canonical [Depot](README.md#Depot)
- supply chain — inscribe [Reliquary](README.md#Reliquary), [Ordain](README.md#Ordain) all [Hallmarks](README.md#Hallmark)
- [Crucible](README.md#Crucible) suite
- cleanup

Outcome:

- Success: green tally and clean exit
- Failure: return to step 2 — never patch-and-continue

```
tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh
```

## 4. Prepare the upstream release

- Claude Code slash command, not a tabtarget — the contribution ceremony is interactive by design
- Drives the upstream contribution review and produces the contribution-ready branch
- Success: the ceremony reports a clean upstream-ready state
- Failure: address findings and re-run; if the underlying issue is structural, return to step 2

```
/rbk-prep-release
```

## 5. Push to the upstream remote

- Universal git operation; no tabtarget
- Pushes the contribution-ready branch produced by step 4
- Success: the upstream accepts the push
- Failure: resolve the upstream-side reason (branch protection, force-push policy, etc.) and retry — the local state from step 4 remains valid

```
git push <upstream> <branch>
```

**1. Branch.** Currently on `main`. Paddock's Release-branch execution contract says rbw-tP must run on a release branch (not main). Existing conventions in the remote suggest names like `bth-heat-BB-*` or `<date>-*`. Need you to pick a branch name (or confirm one of these patterns).

**2. main is +1 ahead of origin.** HEAD is `bc4ed03f jjb:1011-d7127ac5::i: OFFICIUM 260503-1010` — looks like a JJK background officium commit. Two options:
   - Push main first, then branch off
   - Branch off here without pushing main (the JJK officium commit will ride along on the release branch)

Once branch is sorted, the sequence is: confirm Payor health (`rbw-gPR`) → `rbw-MZ` + commit → `rbw-tP`.

Which branch name, and should I push main first?

content.text:
**Surprise #2:** Push rejected — alpha sibling has main checked out, blocks default push. The unpushed-commit guard in `rblm_zero` exists to prevent commit loss, but the local-sibling remote topology fights it.

Options:
1. **Switch alpha's branch.** Change alpha's working tree to a different branch, push, switch back.
2. **Configure alpha `receive.denyCurrentBranch = updateInstead`.** Standard setup for sibling-pair topology; alpha auto-updates working tree on push.
3. **Discard the JJK officium commit on beta.** It's a session-internal commit (from this officium ☉260503-1000); could be reset.
4. **Bypass guard.** Slate as a fixture surprise — the unpushed guard's local-sibling-topology blind spot is worth noting.

I lean (2) — `updateInstead` is the standard fix for paired working copies and is operator-controlled, but it requires you to change alpha. Option (1) is one-shot but disruptive. Which do you want?

content.text:
The remote `origin` is `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster` — a regular non-bare working copy, not a bare repository. That sibling repo currently has `main` checked out.

Git refuses by default because pushing into a checked-out branch of a non-bare repo would advance that repo's `HEAD` while leaving its index and working tree pointing at the old commit — instantly inconsistent state. The remote-side fix would be `git reset --hard` (destructive), so git declines rather than silently corrupting alpha's working tree.

Three resolutions, in order of cleanliness for a paired-working-copy topology like yours:

1. **`git config receive.denyCurrentBranch updateInstead` on alpha.** Alpha auto-updates its working tree on push *iff* alpha's tree is clean. The standard fix for sibling pairs.
2. **Switch alpha to a different branch temporarily.** Then push, then switch alpha back.
3. **Make alpha bare** (drop alpha's working tree). Probably not what you want if alpha is the canonical workspace.

Side note on why the +1 came back after pull: your `git pull --rebase` fast-forwarded beta's main from `bc4ed03f` to `2615224d` (alpha had advanced), then replayed your JJK officium commit on top — putting you back at +1 ahead. The push problem is structural, not transient.

file.content:
buc_require() {
  local z_prompt="$1"
  local z_required_value="$2"

  if test "${BURE_CONFIRM:-}" = "skip"; then
    buc_step "Confirm: (skipped — BURE_CONFIRM=skip)"
    return 0
  fi

  test -z "${BURE_CONFIRM:-}" || buc_die "BURE_CONFIRM must be 'skip' or unset, got '${BURE_CONFIRM}'"

  sleep 1
  printf '%b\n' "${BUC_yellow}${z_prompt}${BUC_reset}" >/dev/tty
  printf 'Type %s to confirm: ' "${z_required_value}" >/dev/tty
  local z_input
  read -r z_input </dev/tty
  test "${z_input}" = "${z_required_value}" || buc_die "Confirmation failed — expected '${z_required_value}', got '${z_input}'"
}

buc_execute() {
  set -e
  local prefix="$1"
  local title="$2"
  local env_func="$3"
  local command="${4:-}"
  shift 3; test -z "${command}" || shift

  export BUC_VERBOSE="${BUC_VERBOSE:-0}"

content.text:
**Surprise #4 (gauntlet failure):** Cross-section interface mismatch between fixture expectation and mantle behavior.

**Where:** `pristine-lifecycle / rbtdrp_sa_cycle` at `rbtdrp_pristine.rs:744-757`

**The mantle does this** (per `rbgp_governor_mantle` stdout): writes RBRA to `../station-files/secrets/assay/rbra.env` (fixed staging path), emits operator advisory `mv assay/rbra.env governor/rbra.env`.

**The fixture expects this** (lines 741-757): `governor-<timestamp>.rbra` deposited in mantle's BURV output dir; reads via `read_dir(mantle.burv_output)` and looks for `governor-*.rbra`. Then it copies to canonical.

**Result:** Mantle succeeded ("Governor reset completed successfully"), governor SA created in cloud, but fixture fails locating the RBRA → fail-fast → suite stops.

**Triage:** This is **not tiny inline** — it's a design-call about which side defines the contract. Two paths:

1. **Mantle is truth, fixture follows.** Update fixture to read `secrets/assay/rbra.env` (the same path the operator's manual install advisory points at) and skip the BURV-output search. Fixture lines 793+ already handle `assay_canonical` for retriever/director invest, so the pattern is already in the file — just inconsistent for the governor case.
2. **Fixture is truth, mantle follows.** Change mantle to *also* deposit `governor-<timestamp>.rbra` in BURV output dir (additive — preserve assay-staging path for human flow).

Path 1 looks cheaper and more consistent with the rest of the fixture (retriever/director invest cases already use assay-staging). Path 2 widens the mantle interface for test-harness convenience.

**Cost note:** Two GCP projects already created (one throwaway pristine depot, plus the canonical from prior runs). Fixing this and re-running mints another throwaway. Per failure-mode contract, recovery is marshal-zero + retry, not patch-and-continue.

**Slate as follow-up pace under ₣BB?** I lean yes — this needs a clean docket and its own commit. Could be silked something like `pristine-mantle-rbra-interface-align`. Want me to slate it, or should we attempt path 1 inline within ₢BBAAX first (treating it as on-the-edge tiny)?

file.content:
if test "${z_key_code}" = "200"; then
      break
    fi

    if test "${z_key_code}" = "404" && test "${z_key_attempt}" -lt "${RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_MAX}"; then
      buc_warn "keys.create returned 404 (SA write-path propagation delay), retry ${z_key_attempt}/${RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_MAX} in ${RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_DELAY_SEC}s..."
      sleep "${RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_DELAY_SEC}"
      continue
    fi

    rbgu_http_require_ok "Generate Governor key" "${z_gov_key_infix}"
  done

  buc_step 'Extract and decode key data'
  local z_key_b64
  z_key_b64=$(rbgu_json_field_capture "${z_gov_key_infix}" '.privateKeyData') \
    || buc_die "Failed to extract privateKeyData"

  # Decode into the assay subdirectory (RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/assay/) so the
  # only readable form of the private key shares lifecycle and location
  # with the final RBRA file — credentials never leak into BURD_TEMP_DIR.
  local -r z_assay_dir="${RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE%/*}"
  local -r z_key_json="${z_assay_dir}/_decoded_governor_key.json"
  printf '%s' "${z_key_b64}" | openssl enc -base64 -d -A > "${z_key_json}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to decode key data"

  buc_step 'Convert JSON key to RBRA format'
  local -r z_rbra_file="${RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE}"

  local z_client_email
  z_client_email=$(jq -r '.client_email' "${z_key_json}") || buc_die "Failed to extract client_email"
  test -n "${z_client_email}" || buc_die "Empty client_email in key JSON"

  local z_private_key
  z_private_key=$(jq -r '.private_key' "${z_key_json}") || buc_die "Failed to extract private_key"
  test -n "${z_private_key}" || buc_die "Empty private_key in key JSON"

  local z_project_id
  z_project_id=$(jq -r '.project_id' "${z_key_json}") || buc_die "Failed to extract project_id"
  test -n "${z_project_id}" || buc_die "Empty project_id in key JSON"

  buc_step 'Write RBRA file' "${z_rbra_file}"
  {
    printf 'RBRA_ROLE=%s\n' "${RBCC_role_governor}"
    printf 'RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL="%s"\n'      "${z_client_email}"
    printf 'RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY="'; printf '%s' "${z_private_key}"; printf '"\n'
    printf 'RBRA_PROJECT_ID="%s"\n'        "${z_project_id}"
    printf 'RBRA_TOKEN_LIFETIME_SEC=1800\n'
  } > "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "Failed to write RBRA file ${z_rbra_file}"

  test -f "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "Failed to write RBRA file ${z_rbra_file}"

  # Decoded JSON lives in RBRR_SECRETS_DIR (not BURD_TEMP_DIR), so
  # BCG:518's no-module-temp-deletion rule does not bind. Remove now
  # that the bytes are persisted in RBRA form.
  rm -f "${z_key_json}" || buc_die "Failed to remove decoded key file: ${z_key_json}"

  buc_success "Governor reset completed successfully"
  buc_info "Governor service account: ${z_governor_email}"
  buc_info "RBRA file written: ${z_rbra_file}"
  buc_info ""
  buc_info "Install the RBRA file:"
  buc_bare "        mv ${z_rbra_file} ${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE}"
}

# eof

content.text:
Now the disconnect is crisp. The mantle uses `${RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE}` (= `secrets/assay/rbra.env`) — flat name, role-marked by directory not by filename. The fictional `governor-*.rbra` pattern in `rbtdrp_pristine.rs:741-764` exists nowhere else in the codebase. Speculative interface, never implemented.

The same fixture, lines 794+, handles the retriever and director invest cases via `assay_canonical` — that's the right pattern. Let me read those to confirm the mirror.

content.text:
Reading the fixture: it invokes `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_DEPOT_LIST` after unmake, reads the per-moniker fact file, expects absent or `DELETE_REQUESTED`. List wrote `COMPLETE`.

But the unmake itself succeeded ("Verify deletion state transition" passed, project status DELETE_REQUESTED). ~12s later the depot list classifies as COMPLETE. Two suspects: GCP eventual-consistency at the projects.list API, or rbgp_depot_list classifying via stale local tracking. Let me check what `rbgp_depot_list` reads.

rbgp_depot_list() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "List all depot instances and their status"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buc_step 'Authenticate as Payor'
  local z_token
  z_token=$(zrbgp_authenticate_capture) || buc_die "Failed to authenticate as Payor via OAuth"

  buc_step 'Probe depot states and emit per-moniker fact files'
  zrbgp_depot_state_emit "${z_token}"

  buc_step 'Render depot summary'
  local z_total_count=0
  local z_complete_count=0
  local z_delete_requested_count=0

  buc_info ""
  buc_info "=== DEPOT SUMMARY ==="
  printf "%-40s %s\n" "PROJECT_ID" "STATUS"

  # Per-iteration synthesized locals (BCG exception 2)
  local z_fact_path=""
  local z_basename=""
  local z_moniker=""
  local z_project_id=""
  local z_project_fact_path=""
  local z_state=""

  # Walk emitted depot fact files; stem is the moniker, content is the state.
  # The sidecar depot-project fact file carries the canonical project_id.
  shopt -s nullglob
  for z_fact_path in "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"/*.${RBCC_fact_ext_depot}; do
    z_basename="${z_fact_path##*/}"
    z_moniker="${z_basename%.${RBCC_fact_ext_depot}}"
    z_project_fact_path="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${z_moniker}.${RBCC_fact_ext_depot_project}"
    z_state=$(<"${z_fact_path}")
    test -n "${z_state}" || buc_die "Empty state in fact file: ${z_fact_path}"
    test -f "${z_project_fact_path}" || buc_die "Missing depot-project fact file: ${z_project_fact_path}"
    z_project_id=$(<"${z_project_fact_path}")
    test -n "${z_project_id}" || buc_die "Empty project_id in fact file: ${z_project_fact_path}"

    printf "%-40s %s\n" "${z_project_id}" "${z_state}"
    z_total_count=$((z_total_count + 1))
    case "${z_state}" in
      "${RBGP_DEPOT_STATE_COMPLETE}")          z_complete_count=$((z_complete_count + 1)) ;;
      "${RBGP_DEPOT_STATE_DELETE_REQUESTED}")  z_delete_requested_count=$((z_delete_requested_count + 1)) ;;
    esac
  done
  shopt -u nullglob

  if test "${z_total_count}" -eq 0; then
    buc_info "No depot projects found"
    return 0
  fi

  buc_info ""
  buc_info "=== SUMMARY ==="
  buc_info "Total depots:     ${z_total_count}"
  buc_info "Complete:         ${z_complete_count}"
  buc_info "Delete-requested: ${z_delete_requested_count}"
}

rbgp_payor_oauth_refresh() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "Refresh expired OAuth credentials following RBAGS manual procedure"
  buc_doc_lines "Use this when OAuth tokens expire after 6 months or are compromised"
  buc_doc_lines "Requires downloading new OAuth JSON from Google Cloud Console"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buc_step 'Display OAuth refresh procedure'
  buc_info ""
  buc_info "=== Manual Payor OAuth Refresh Procedure ==="
  buc_info ""
  buc_info "OAuth credentials need to be refreshed. Follow these steps:"
  buc_info ""
  buc_info "1. Navigate to APIs & Services > Credentials in Payor Project"
  buc_info "   Console URL: https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials?project=${RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID}"
  buc_info ""
  buc_info "2. Find existing 'Recipe Bottle Payor' OAuth client"
  buc_info ""  
  buc_info "3. Download new JSON credentials:"
  buc_info "   - Click the download icon next to the OAuth client"
  buc_info "   - Or regenerate client secret if compromised"
  buc_info ""
  date +%Y%m%d > "${ZRBGP_SCRATCH_FILE}" 2>/dev/null || true
  local z_today_stamp=$(<"${ZRBGP_SCRATCH_FILE}")
  buc_info "4. Save as timestamped file:"

file.content:
zrbgp_depot_state_emit() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_token="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_token}" || buc_die "zrbgp_depot_state_emit: token required"

  local -r z_search_query="displayName:${RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX}*"
  local -r z_search_url_base="${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V3}/projects:search"

  # Anchor prefix string used inside loop for displayName stripping.
  local -r z_display_prefix="${RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX} "

  # Per-iteration synthesized locals (BCG exception 2 — declare outside, assign inside)
  local z_search_page=1
  local z_search_page_token=""
  local z_search_url=""
  local z_query_enc=""
  local z_tok_enc=""
  local z_search_infix=""
  local z_project_count=0
  local z_index=0
  local z_project_id=""
  local z_display_name=""
  local z_crm_state=""
  local z_state=""
  local z_moniker=""

  z_query_enc=$(rbgu_urlencode_capture "${z_search_query}") \
    || buc_die "Failed to URL-encode CRM v3 search query"

  while :; do
    z_search_url="${z_search_url_base}?query=${z_query_enc}"
    if test -n "${z_search_page_token}"; then
      z_tok_enc=$(rbgu_urlencode_capture "${z_search_page_token}") \
        || buc_die "Failed to URL-encode CRM v3 pageToken"
      z_search_url="${z_search_url}&pageToken=${z_tok_enc}"
    fi

    z_search_infix="depot_state_search_${z_search_page}"
    rbgu_http_json "GET" "${z_search_url}" "${z_token}" "${z_search_infix}"
    rbgu_http_require_ok "CRM v3 projects:search" "${z_search_infix}"

    z_project_count=$(rbgu_json_field_capture "${z_search_infix}" '.projects // [] | length') \
      || z_project_count=0

    z_index=0
    while test "${z_index}" -lt "${z_project_count}"; do
      z_crm_state=$(rbgu_json_field_capture "${z_search_infix}" ".projects[${z_index}].state") \
        || { z_index=$((z_index + 1)); continue; }

      if test "${z_crm_state}" = "DELETE_REQUESTED"; then
        z_state="${RBGP_DEPOT_STATE_DELETE_REQUESTED}"
      elif test "${z_crm_state}" = "ACTIVE"; then
        z_state="${RBGP_DEPOT_STATE_COMPLETE}"
      else
        buc_log_args "Skipping project with unrecognized state: ${z_crm_state}"
        z_index=$((z_index + 1))
        continue
      fi

      z_project_id=$(rbgu_json_field_capture "${z_search_infix}" ".projects[${z_index}].projectId") \
        || { z_index=$((z_index + 1)); continue; }

      z_display_name=$(rbgu_json_field_capture "${z_search_infix}" ".projects[${z_index}].displayName") \
        || { z_index=$((z_index + 1)); continue; }

      # Strip the anchor prefix (including trailing space) to get the moniker.
      # displayName format: "${RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX} <moniker>"
      if [[ "${z_display_name}" != "${z_display_prefix}"* ]]; then
        buc_log_args "Skipping project — displayName does not match anchor: ${z_display_name}"
        z_index=$((z_index + 1))
        continue
      fi
      z_moniker="${z_display_name#"${z_display_prefix}"}"

content.text:
The failure mechanism is now clear:

**Root cause:** GCP API eventual-consistency mismatch between two paths.

- `rbgp_depot_unmake` → calls `projects.delete`, then verifies via `projects.get` (strongly consistent — saw `DELETE_REQUESTED` immediately).
- `rbgp_depot_list` → uses `projects:search` via `zrbgp_depot_state_emit` at `rbgp_Payor.sh:202`, mapping `state=="ACTIVE"→COMPLETE` and `state=="DELETE_REQUESTED"→DELETE_REQUESTED`. The search index lags `projects.delete` by some seconds; ~12s later the search still returned `ACTIVE`.

The fixture (`rbtdrp_depot_tear_down_impl`) verifies via list (search-based path), which is exactly the lagging endpoint.

**Triage — three options:**

1. **Tiny inline (fixture-side retry).** Add a poll loop in `rbtdrp_depot_tear_down_impl`: invoke list, check fact file, retry every ~5s up to ~60-90s until state transitions to `DELETE_REQUESTED` or fact file disappears. ~15 lines added. Doesn't touch production code; treats search-vs-get lag as inherent.

2. **Production fix.** Change `zrbgp_depot_state_emit` to use `projects.get` (strongly consistent) instead of/in addition to search. Wider blast radius — list semantics change. Fixes the real consistency disagreement between unmake's verify path and list's classification path.

3. **Slate as follow-up pace under ₣BB.** Surface as a design call: do we accept search-lag in list (and make tests poll), or do we make list strongly consistent? The locked design decisions in the paddock include depot identity collapse but not list-vs-get consistency policy.

This is closer to "design call" than "tiny inline" per docket — the search-vs-get split is a real infrastructure fact that's never been contended with at this layer. **I lean (3)**: slate `pristine-tear-down-list-consistency` as a follow-up under ₣BB, leave ₢BBAAX open until that pace lands and the gauntlet runs green. (1) is reasonable as a holdover if you want to keep moving.

Cost note: 4 prior canc-d depots + 2 prlc-d depots from this run = 6 pending-delete projects accumulating. Each re-run mints another throwaway prlc-d.

Which path?

content: Mounting ₣BB ₢BBAAX (rbw-tP first-run gauntlet), aiming for green tally. First run's governor-RBRA fixture bug fixed and re-run failed at depot-tear-down on a GCP search-vs-get consistency lag. Next action: pick fixture-retry, production-fix, or slate-followup. (disable recaps in /config)

Based on the search results, here's information about GCP Cloud Resource Manager's eventual consistency behavior and DELETE_REQUESTED state handling:

## Eventual Consistency with projects.search/projects.list

The List method is eventually consistent with project mutations; this means that a newly created project may not appear in the results or recent updates to an existing project may not be reflected in the results. To retrieve the latest state of a project, use the GetProject method.

The documentation notes that in future versions of this API, this List method will be split into List and Search to properly capture the behavioral difference.

## DELETE_REQUESTED State

When a project is deleted using projects.delete:

- This method changes the Project's lifecycle state from ACTIVE to DELETE_REQUESTED. The deletion starts at an unspecified time, at which point the Project is no longer accessible.

- Until the deletion completes, you can check the lifecycle state checked by retrieving the Project with GetProject, and the Project remains visible to ListProjects.

- After the deletion completes, the Project is not retrievable by the GetProject and ListProjects methods.

- The project remains in a DELETE_REQUESTED state for 30 days. During this period, project owners can restore the project.

## Consistency Guarantees

If the request filter contains a `parent.type` and `parent.id` and the caller has the `resourcemanager.projects.list` permission on the parent, the results will be drawn from an alternate index which provides more consistent results.

results:
Based on the search results, here's information about GCP Cloud Resource Manager's eventual consistency behavior and DELETE_REQUESTED state handling:

## Eventual Consistency with projects.search/projects.list

The List method is eventually consistent with project mutations; this means that a newly created project may not appear in the results or recent updates to an existing project may not be reflected in the results. To retrieve the latest state of a project, use the GetProject method.

The documentation notes that in future versions of this API, this List method will be split into List and Search to properly capture the behavioral difference.

## DELETE_REQUESTED State

When a project is deleted using projects.delete:

- This method changes the Project's lifecycle state from ACTIVE to DELETE_REQUESTED. The deletion starts at an unspecified time, at which point the Project is no longer accessible.

- Until the deletion completes, you can check the lifecycle state checked by retrieving the Project with GetProject, and the Project remains visible to ListProjects.

- After the deletion completes, the Project is not retrievable by the GetProject and ListProjects methods.

- The project remains in a DELETE_REQUESTED state for 30 days. During this period, project owners can restore the project.

## Consistency Guarantees

If the request filter contains a `parent.type` and `parent.id` and the caller has the `resourcemanager.projects.list` permission on the parent, the results will be drawn from an alternate index which provides more consistent results.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about the GCP Cloud Resource Manager v3 `projects.search` method and its eventual consistency characteristics:

## Key Findings:

**Eventual Consistency Lag:**
The `search_projects` method is eventually consistent with project mutations; this means that a newly created project may not appear in the results or recent updates to an existing project may not be reflected in the results.

**Specific Behavior with Deleted Projects:**
After the deletion completes, the project is not retrievable by the GetProject, ListProjects, and SearchProjects methods. However, the search method may omit some resources from the search result, particularly any resources that have been changed recently.

**Important Context:**
The documentation notes that if your scan is intended to search for a resource that was created some time ago, you can perform a faster scan that has eventual consistency rather than strong consistency. This indicates that the `search` method is optimized for scenarios where you're not looking for very recently modified resources.

The search results don't specify an exact lag duration for consistency in the `projects.search` v3 API, but they emphasize that recently changed resources (including deletions) may not immediately appear in search results due to the eventual consistency model.

lastPrompt: Websearch to increase confidence that you have diagnosed the issue soundly

content.text:
Diagnosis confirmed by Google's documentation — high confidence:

**From Google's Cloud Resource Manager docs:**

1. **`SearchProjects` (v3) is explicitly eventually consistent with project mutations.** "Recent updates to an existing project may not be reflected in the results. To retrieve the latest state of a project, use the GetProject method."

2. **The deletion state transition is the canonical case of this lag:** "Until the deletion completes, you can check the lifecycle state by retrieving the Project with GetProject" — the strongly-consistent path. "The search method may omit some resources from the search result, particularly any resources that have been changed recently."

3. **Documented design trade-off, not a bug:** "If your scan is intended to search for a resource that was created some time ago, you can perform a faster scan that has eventual consistency rather than strong consistency."

This matches our observed behavior precisely:
- `rbgp_depot_unmake` calls `projects.delete`, then verifies via direct project lookup (strongly consistent — saw `DELETE_REQUESTED` immediately).
- `zrbgp_depot_state_emit` (`rbgp_Payor.sh:202`) uses `projects:search` — Google's eventually-consistent path. ~12s after the delete request, search still returned `state=ACTIVE`.

The diagnosis stands: it's GCP's documented search-vs-get consistency split, not an RBK bug per se. The unmake operation is correct; the list operation is consistent with documented GCP behavior; the fixture's expectation that they agree within 12 seconds is the actual mismatch.

This sharpens the triage. Now option (2) ("production fix: use projects.get in zrbgp_depot_state_emit") is harder than I initially framed — list-as-search is intentional for browse-many-projects performance; switching to per-project `get` calls inflates request count by N. The cleaner production move would be a hybrid: search for discovery, then refine via get for any project whose state we need to be authoritative about (or just for recently-modified ones).

My recommendation strengthens to **(3) slate as follow-up pace**. The depot list's eventual-consistency posture is a real product decision deserving its own docket, not an inline fix.

For ₢BBAAX progress: this surface earns a new pace under ₣BB; ₢BBAAX stays open until that pace lands and the gauntlet runs green. Want me to slate it?

Sources:
- [Delete and restore projects | Google Cloud Resource Manager](https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/delete-restore-projects)
- [Method: projects.delete | Resource Manager v1](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/reference/rest/v1/projects/delete)
- [Method: projects.undelete | Resource Manager v3](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/reference/rest/v3/projects/undelete)
- [Class ProjectsClient (Resource Manager v3 Python)](https://docs.cloud.google.com/python/docs/reference/cloudresourcemanager/latest/google.cloud.resourcemanager_v3.services.projects.ProjectsClient)
- [Troubleshooting project deletion | Google Cloud](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/troubleshooting-project-deletion)

fi

  buc_step 'Verify deletion state transition'
  local -r z_max_attempts=12
  local z_attempt=1
  local z_final_state
  
  while test "${z_attempt}" -le "${z_max_attempts}"; do
    sleep 5
    buc_log_args "Checking deletion state (attempt ${z_attempt}/${z_max_attempts})"
    
    rbgu_http_json "GET" "${z_project_info_url}" "${z_token}" "depot_destroy_state_check"

    local z_state_check_code
    z_state_check_code=$(rbgu_http_code_capture "depot_destroy_state_check") || z_state_check_code=""
    if test "${z_state_check_code}" = "200"; then
      z_final_state=$(rbgu_json_field_capture "depot_destroy_state_check" '.state // "UNKNOWN"') || z_final_state="UNKNOWN"

      if test "${z_final_state}" = "DELETE_REQUESTED"; then
        break
      fi
    fi
    
    z_attempt=$((z_attempt + 1))
  done
  
  if test "${z_final_state}" != "DELETE_REQUESTED"; then
    buc_die "Failed to verify deletion state transition. Current state: ${z_final_state}"
  fi

  buc_step 'Update depot tracking'
  zrbgp_depot_list_update || buc_log_args "Warning: Failed to update depot tracking after deletion"

  # Success
  buc_success "Depot ${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID} successfully marked for deletion"

zrbgp_depot_list_update() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  buc_log_args "Refreshing per-moniker depot fact files"

  local z_token
  z_token=$(zrbgp_authenticate_capture) || buc_die "Failed to authenticate for depot fact refresh"

  zrbgp_depot_state_emit "${z_token}"
}

######################################################################
# External Functions (rbgp_*)

zrbgp_billing_attach() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_billing_account="${1:-}"

  buc_doc_brief "Attach a billing account to the project"
  buc_doc_param "billing_account" "Billing account ID to attach"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${z_billing_account}" || buc_die "Billing account ID required"

  buc_step "Attaching billing account: ${z_billing_account}"

  local z_token
  z_token=$(rbgu_get_governor_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to get admin token"
  local -r z_billing_body="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_billing_attach.json"
  jq -n --arg billingAccountName "billingAccounts/${z_billing_account}" \

content.text:
Verified. Three things I'd asserted on faith now have evidence:

**1. Unmake's verify-deletion path** (`rbgp_Payor.sh:1052-1078`): Polls CRM v3 `projects.get` (URL built from line 909-910, hit at line 1061) every 5 seconds up to 12 attempts. Exits only when GET returns `state == DELETE_REQUESTED`. Strongly consistent. So when unmake's stdout said "Verify deletion state transition" passed and exited 0, the state had genuinely transitioned at the strongly-consistent endpoint.

**2. Unmake's "Update depot tracking" step** (`rbgp_Payor.sh:1080-1081`) calls `zrbgp_depot_list_update`, which (line 261-270) is a five-line wrapper that calls **the same `zrbgp_depot_state_emit` search path**. So that step is search-based too — and it writes into the **unmake's** `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR`, not the list's.

**3. BURD_OUTPUT_DIR is per-invocation** (`bud_dispatch.sh:120-127`): cleared with `rm -rf` at every tabtarget entry. The fact files written during unmake's tracking-update don't persist to the next invocation. The fixture reads `list_after.burv_output` — that's the **list's** fresh output, populated by a fresh search query.

**Net mechanism:**

- GCP-side: project is in `DELETE_REQUESTED` (confirmed by unmake's GET-polling).
- ~12 seconds later, fixture invokes list. List runs `projects:search`. Search index hasn't propagated the state change yet, returns `state=ACTIVE`. `zrbgp_depot_state_emit` maps that to `RBGP_DEPOT_STATE_COMPLETE`, writes to fact file. Fixture reads "COMPLETE", fails.

The unmake's tracking-update step is, from the fixture's perspective, dead — different BURV scope, never read. The fixture's verification path is the list's fresh search, which structurally lags the GET-polling that unmake itself does.

What I'd misframed before: I implied the unmake's tracking-update was more authoritative state somehow. It isn't — it's the same eventually-consistent search path, just with an earlier timestamp into a directory the fixture doesn't look at. The unmake genuinely waited up to 60s for GET to confirm DELETE_REQUESTED; the fixture's later list call doesn't get that benefit.

Stopping there — that's the technical picture I can stand behind.

message.content: All right that sounds significantly more focused on the problem.  And yet it still isn't clear if you are specifying a solution, and how it is different than the failure we just had?

content.text:
Honest answer: I haven't specified a solution. I sketched mitigations and called one of them "tiny inline" — but they don't all address the failure mechanism, and the one that does is a production-code change, not a fixture tweak.

Concretely, against what just failed:

**Fixture-side retry loop** (the option I was loosely calling "tiny inline" before): re-invokes list every N seconds until state transitions or fact file disappears. Same API path under the hood — `projects:search` — so it doesn't fix the consistency problem; it just budgets more wall-clock for search-index propagation. **Failure mode is identical to what just happened**, just with a higher wait threshold. And the lag isn't documented to have any specific bound — Google's docs say "may be omitted" without a time guarantee. So any retry budget I pick is a guess about a window I don't know. On a bad day this re-fails the same way; on a good day it adds steady-state wait cost to every successful run.

**Production change to `zrbgp_depot_state_emit`** — search to discover candidates, then `projects.get` per candidate for authoritative state — would actually move us off the failure mode. Same operator-facing list output, but the state column would now reflect the strongly-consistent endpoint. Any caller (fixture or human) sees the post-`projects.delete` state immediately, because we'd no longer be querying the eventually-consistent index. **This is what's structurally different from what just happened**: the read path changes, not the wait budget.

**Skipping list-after-unmake**: not a solution — it's removing the assertion that's failing. Doesn't address anything; weakens the test.

So my actual position: the only candidate I trust to keep the gauntlet from re-failing on the next run is the production-code change. The fixture-retry idea I floated is a flake mitigation, not a fix, and I shouldn't have framed it the way I did.

lastPrompt: All right that sounds significantly more focused on the problem.  And yet it still isn't clear if you are specifying a solution, and how it is different than the failure we just had?

message.content: Please now state precisely toe producion change you are proposing to address this failure now.

content.text:
The production change is in **one function**: `zrbgp_depot_state_emit` in `Tools/rbk/rbgp_Payor.sh` (definition at line 163, classification block at lines 209-220).

**What it does today:** paginated `projects:search` against CRM v3, reads `state` directly from the search hit (`.projects[i].state` at line 210), maps `ACTIVE→COMPLETE` / `DELETE_REQUESTED→DELETE_REQUESTED` / else→skip.

**What I'm proposing:** keep the search call for discovery (we still need to find depot projects by display-name prefix; we don't have project IDs ahead of time), but stop trusting the search hit's `state`. After extracting `projectId` from the hit, issue a `GET ${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V3}/projects/${projectId}` — the same URL pattern already used at line 909-910 by the unmake — and read `.state` from the GET response. Then run the existing classification on that state.

GET handling:
- `200` → use its `.state` for classification.
- `404` → project completed deletion between the search call and the GET. Skip the iteration; no fact file emitted (the fixture's logic at `rbtdrp_pristine.rs:1037-1039` already treats absent fact file as success for tear-down).
- other → log and skip, mirroring the existing "unrecognized state" handling.

**What changes for callers:** all three callers (`rbgp_depot_list`, `zrbgp_depot_list_update` invoked by depot_create, `zrbgp_depot_list_update` invoked by depot_unmake) emit fact files derived from the strongly-consistent endpoint instead of the eventually-consistent one. Operator-facing list output is identical in shape; only the freshness guarantee improves.

**Cost:** today, N depots = 1 search call (one page until ~50). After the change, N depots = 1 search call + N GET calls. For 4-10 depots that's 5-11 HTTP calls instead of 1; no concern.

**Why this is structurally different from the retry loop:** the retry loop keeps querying the same lagging index, gambling on propagation within an undocumented window. This change reads from the endpoint that `projects.delete` mutates synchronously — the same endpoint the unmake itself polls for its verify-deletion step (line 1061). The fixture's list-after-unmake assertion will see exactly what the unmake's verify saw, with no race window between them.

That's the change. Want me to apply it?

message.content: Not quite yet.  We have several similar handlers and tolerances in this codebase.  Please survey them and get very clear whether we should have a bespoke handler here or if there is an excellent reuse candidate?

content.text: Good call. Surveying.
